passages.author: Marcus Aurelius
passages.text: Begin the morning by saying to thyself, I shall meet with the busy-body, the ungrateful, arrogant, deceitful, envious, unsocial. All these things happen to them by reason of their ignorance of what is good and evil. We are made for co-operation, like feet, like hands, like eyelids, like the rows of the upper and lower teeth. To act against one another then is contrary to nature; and it is acting against one another to be vexed and to turn away.
passages.context: The opening of Book 2, written in camp; Marcus prepares for difficult people by recalling that humans are made for cooperation.

passages.author: Marcus Aurelius
passages.text: If our intellectual part is common, the reason also, in respect of which we are rational beings, is common: if this is so, common also is the reason which commands us what to do, and what not to do; if this is so, there is a common law also; if this is so, we are fellow-citizens; if this is so, we are members of some political community; if this is so, the world is in a manner a state.
passages.context: Marcus reasons step by step to the cosmopolitan conclusion that the world is a single commonwealth.

passages.author: Marcus Aurelius
passages.text: As the members of one body, so rational beings in their separate states constituted to co-operate in one work. The perception of this will be more apparent to thee if thou often sayest to thyself, I am a member of the system of rational beings. But if thou sayest that thou art a part, thou dost not yet love men from thy heart; beneficence does not yet delight thee for its own sake; thou still doest it merely as a thing of propriety, and not yet as doing good to thyself.
passages.context: Marcus draws the distinction between being a mere 'part' and being a living 'member' of the body of rational beings.
